abstract: Establishing a clear formation mechanism for carbon dots (CDs) remains a significant challenge in nanotechnology. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal treatment of 4-aminoantipyrine, yields blue-fluorescent nanoparticles (~5 nm) with high water solubility and gram-scale productivity. The structural evolution and growth mechanisms were systematically monitored across various reaction times (3 to 21 hours) using FT-IR, $^{13}$C-NMR, $^1$H-NMR, and UV-visible spectroscopy. Spectroscopic evidence reveals that extended hydrothermal treatment leads to the degradation of the precursor’s aromatic signals and the simultaneous emergence of aliphatic and carbonyl functional groups. This transformation is driven by the hydrolysis of the pyrazole ring and noncovalent $\pi$–$\pi$ stacking interactions of aromatic residues, which facilitate core formation. As the reaction progresses, the surface becomes increasingly enriched with polar functional groups, resulting in a significant enhancement of the photoluminescent quantum yield. HR-TEM and XRD analysis confirm the production of high-purity NCDs with an amorphous turbostratic carbon phase and a lattice d-spacing of 0.26 nm. These insights provide a deeper understanding of how synthesis duration dictates the chemical and optical properties of NCDs, offering a cost-effective pathway for optimizing high-quality carbonaceous nanomaterials.
